questions.prompt: Think of the last significant strategic decision in your firm that was challenged by someone with less formal authority, lower status, or weaker political position than the decision-maker. What primarily determined the outcome of that challenge?

directObservationGate.prompt: Direct Observation Gate — have you personally observed this in your firm in the last 24 months?

options.text: The quality of the argument. If the challenger's case was analytically stronger, the decision was revised — regardless of relative status.
The seniority or status of the decision-maker. The challenge was heard, considered, and ultimately did not change the outcome. Position governs.
Who had more leverage or visibility at that moment — not status, not argument quality, but demonstrated control over resources, narrative, or commercial exposure.
Whether the challenge aligned with the firm's established direction or investment thesis. Challenges that supported the prevailing thesis were heard; those that did not were dismissed.
I cannot answer from direct observation.

methodologyNotes.text: ◆ Cognitive interview note (Willis CI): Sharpest discriminator for NT/STJ (A): argument quality overrides status. v3.1 broadens "below the decision-maker in the formal hierarchy" to "less formal authority, lower status, or weaker political position" — works across founder-led, flat, and partnership organisations.

◇ Evidence architecture note (v3.1): Direct-observation gate compatible. v3.1 stem broadening absorbs the agent critique that strict hierarchical framing did not fit smaller firms or partnership structures.

questions.prompt: When a senior person at or near your own level failed to deliver an important commitment, how was responsibility actually handled in the last instance you observed?

directObservationGate.prompt: Direct Observation Gate — have you personally observed this in your firm in the last 24 months?

options.text: The same formal process applied. Review, documented consequences, or exit — applied at senior level exactly as it would be at junior level.

The miss was openly acknowledged but handled informally through relationships and status. There was no formal consequence; the situation was reframed as a strategic adjustment or change in market conditions.
Responsibility was redistributed downward or sideways to people with less power. The senior person retained position by transferring exposure.
The performance framework does not apply at senior level in the same way. Senior accountability is measured differently — by commercial output, political loyalty, or longer time horizons.
I cannot answer from direct observation.

methodologyNotes.text: ◆ Cognitive interview note (Willis CI): NT/STJ is the only environment where A is realistic at senior level. This is the single most reliable acquirer environment discriminator.
◇ Evidence architecture note (v3.1): v3 rewrite preserved. Direct-observation gate compatible. Option C explicitly captures responsibility-shedding.

questions.prompt: A member of your deal team or investment committee raises a substantive analytical objection to a transaction that has already received tentative leadership approval. What happens?

directObservationGate.prompt: Direct Observation Gate — have you personally observed this in your firm in the last 24 months?

options.text: The objection is taken seriously on its merits. If it holds up under scrutiny, it changes the outcome — regardless of how late in the process.
The objection is heard. But the process has its own momentum. The transaction proceeds unless the objection surfaces something that makes it impossible to proceed.
The person who raises it is noted as someone who does not understand how decisions work here. The objection does not stop the deal.
The objection is absorbed and reframed by leadership as a risk flag to manage, not a reason to reconsider. The transaction proceeds with the objection logged.
I cannot answer from direct observation.

methodologyNotes.text: ◆ Cognitive interview note (Willis CI): Options C and D indicate the highest-risk acquirer environments for target integration.
◇ Evidence architecture note (v3.1): Direct-observation gate compatible. E option absorbs respondents without recent IC-level observation event.

questions.prompt: Your firm has an established way of operating — a process, governance mechanism, or analytical framework that has delivered results. Someone proposes replacing it with an unproven alternative. What actually determines whether it is adopted?

directObservationGate.prompt: Direct Observation Gate — have you personally observed this in your firm in the last 24 months?

options.text: It is piloted in a structured way. If the pilot produces measurable evidence that it outperforms the existing approach, it replaces it.
It is tested immediately in a live context. If it works faster or better in practice, it replaces the old approach. The test IS the decision.
It depends on who proposes it and their standing in the organisation. A trusted senior voice can move it through. An outsider or junior voice cannot.
It is unlikely to be adopted unless it solves an immediate operational problem. The existing approach works. The bar for replacement is high.
I cannot answer from direct observation.

methodologyNotes.text: ◆ Cognitive interview note (Willis CI): A vs B distinction (NT/STJ vs NT/STP) is the critical pair for acquirers most likely to acquire NT/STP targets.
◇ Evidence architecture note (v3.1): Direct-observation gate compatible.

questions.prompt: Your firm has discretionary capacity this year — time, budget, and senior attention not committed to existing mandates. Where, in practice, did most of it go?

directObservationGate.prompt: Direct Observation Gate — have you personally observed this in your firm in the last 24 months?

options.text: To the people and teams with the strongest track record of measurable results in the current period. Performance data drives allocation.
To whoever is generating the most visible activity and momentum at this moment. Current visibility matters more than historical performance.
To the most senior and longest-established people and relationships first. The internal hierarchy determines access. Performance modulates within the hierarchy, not across it.
According to the agreed strategic priorities and direction of the organisation. Allocation follows the plan, not individuals.
I cannot answer from direct observation.

methodologyNotes.text: ◆ Cognitive interview note (Willis CI): Options C and D are most common in PE environments.
◇ Evidence architecture note (v3.1): Direct-observation gate compatible. "In practice" anchor distinguishes espoused from observed allocation.

questions.prompt: A high-performing senior person seriously considered leaving or actually left in the recent past. What factor most strongly determined the outcome?

directObservationGate.prompt: Direct Observation Gate — have you personally observed this in your firm in the last 24 months?

options.text: The quality of the work, intellectual environment, and people they worked with.
Financial structure, carry, vesting, guarantees, or other economic lock-ins that made leaving structurally costly.
Personal loyalty, relationships, obligations, or informal commitments inside the firm.
Power, title, autonomy, political protection, or access to decision-makers — the structural advantages they would lose by leaving.
External factors outside the firm: relocation, family pressure, health, immigration status, or risk-management considerations (fear of removal, exposure, or an internal issue becoming visible).
options.scoringNote: — (no environment signal; flagged external_or_personal — preserved for divergence)

options.text: I cannot answer from direct observation.

methodologyNotes.text: ◆ Cognitive interview note (Willis CI): v3.1: option E added per agent critique. Captures relocation, family, fear of removal, exposure risk — central to Panin's original critique. These motivations carry no environment signal but are recorded for analyst review of overall departure pattern.

◇ Evidence architecture note (v3.1): v3.1 expansion. Six options total (A–F). Letter F now serves the "cannot answer" function. New option E carries no scoring weight but prevents respondents being forced into a false structural answer.

questions.prompt: When your firm faces a significant strategic disagreement internally — two credible views on how to proceed — what resolution mechanism actually applies?

directObservationGate.prompt: Direct Observation Gate — have you personally observed this in your firm in the last 24 months?

options.text: Structured analytical process: both positions are modelled, relevant experts are consulted, and a formal recommendation is produced.
A fast empirical test. Both positions are run in real conditions over a defined short period, and the one that produces better measurable results wins.
Extended debate until one position is clearly more correct. Speed is explicitly subordinated to epistemic quality.
The most senior or most trusted voice resolves it. Analysis informs but does not determine — authority makes the final call.
I cannot answer from direct observation.

methodologyNotes.text: ◆ Cognitive interview note (Willis CI): Critical confirmatory for PE acquirers.
◇ Evidence architecture note (v3.1): Both A and B are flattering self-descriptions for analytically-strong firms — respondent must distinguish on what actually happens.

questions.prompt: During the most recent period of significant commercial pressure on your firm — pressure to reduce costs, increase returns, or restructure — what concrete actions were actually taken regarding people costs, leadership protection, promotions, hiring, or retention? If your firm has not experienced such a period in the last 24 months, select option F.

directObservationGate.prompt: Direct Observation Gate — have you personally observed this in your firm in the last 24 months?

options.text: People investments — development, wellbeing, relationships — were protected even when they did not directly contribute to short-term commercial recovery.
People investments were maintained where they had clear ROI or were operationally critical. Cost discipline applied selectively.
Cost reduction dominated: headcount, bonuses, development budgets, or support functions were cut where the financial case demanded.
The period became an alignment test. People aligned with leadership direction were protected; dissenters or skeptics were exposed.
I observed the period but cannot determine what actions were taken from direct observation.
My firm has not experienced a significant commercial-pressure period in the last 24 months.

methodologyNotes.text: ◆ Cognitive interview note (Willis CI): v3.1: option F added per agent critique. Distinguishes "no such event occurred" from "I cannot answer." Both are diagnostically meaningful but structurally different.
◇ Evidence architecture note (v3.1): v3.1 expansion. Letter F captures structural absence of the precondition. Letter E captures respondent-side observation gap. The two states must not be conflated.

questions.prompt: Think of the last person who left your firm who was widely regarded as high-performing, hard to replace, or commercially important. What was the actual mechanism of their departure?

directObservationGate.prompt: Direct Observation Gate — have you personally observed this in your firm in the last 24 months?

options.text: They left for a clearly better opportunity and the firm accepted it without major conflict.
They left because their growth, autonomy, or influence was blocked internally.
They were informally pushed out, politically isolated, or denied support before leaving.
They left after a conflict over values, leadership style, decision rights, or strategy.
No such departure has occurred in my observation in the recent past.
Such a departure occurred, but I do not know the actual mechanism from direct observation.

methodologyNotes.text: ◆ Cognitive interview note (Willis CI): v3.1: option E split into E and F per agent critique. The two states are diagnostically distinct: "no such departure" indicates strong retention, low transparency, or low recognition of talent; "mechanism unknown" indicates limited respondent access or political shielding.
◇ Evidence architecture note (v3.1): v3.1 split. Both E and F exclude the answer from primary scoring but carry different reliability flags for analyst review.

questions.prompt: Has there been a recent case (last 24 months) where rigorous internal analysis challenged a core assumption, investment thesis, strategy, or leadership belief? directObservationGate.prompt: Direct Observation Gate — have you personally observed this in your firm in the last 24 months?

options.text: YES, and the analysis prompted a genuine revision of the assumption. Belief moved with evidence.
YES, but the analysis was acknowledged and absorbed into a refined version of the original thesis without core change.
YES, but the analysis was dismissed, deprioritised, or the analyst's standing was questioned.

NO such recent case has occurred in my observation, OR I cannot determine whether such a case occurred.

The work itself. Problems are genuinely interesting, the environment develops their capability, and the standards are high.
